ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce; closest parent 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked sea bass generically; no specific code exists for deep-fried preparation with sweet and sour sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce as a prepared dish.

allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce or cornstarch)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce is approximately 400 g, comprising one whole or large fillet portion of fried fish topped generously with sweet and sour sauce and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159) combined with estimation for deep-frying oil absorption and sweet-sour sauce components using USDA SR Legacy sweet and sour sauce data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central cooked sea bass (FDC 175159) as the protein/fat base, applying a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor of approximately 8–10% additional fat per 100 g of fish (per USDA frying methodology), and incorporating sweet and sour sauce macros from USDA SR Legacy sauce data. The composite dish is estimated at approximately 60% fish and 40% sauce/vegetables by cooked weight. Values represent a best-estimate composite and are flagged as medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Sweet and sour sauce / mixed fish dish analogue.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant glycemic driver being the sweet and sour sauce, which contains sugar and starch thickener. Sweet and sour sauce analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) indicate GI values in the 50–60 range for sugar-based sauces with starch. The fish component itself has negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 43.2 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 23.8, rounded to 23.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and sweet and sour sauce components
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving, as a 400 g restaurant portion may deliver approximately 1520 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (23.6) and significant sugar content from sweet and sour sauce, despite a low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the high sugar load in the sweet and sour sauce; requesting reduced sauce or substituting with a lower-sugar preparation can help manage postprandial blood glucose, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; however, sea bass provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that while sea bass provides heart-beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fats, the deep-frying method significantly increases total fat and caloric content; choosing steamed or grilled preparations would be preferable, and sodium from the sauce should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Sea Bass with Sweet and Sour Sauce is a popular Thai seafood dish in which a whole sea bass or large fillet is coated lightly in seasoned cornstarch or flour and deep-fried in hot oil until the skin is crispy and golden. The fish is then topped or served alongside a vibrant sweet and sour sauce made from sugar, vinegar, tomato sauce or ketchup, fish sauce, garlic, onion, bell peppers, and often pineapple chunks, creating a glossy, tangy-sweet glaze. The dish is a staple of Thai restaurant menus across Southeast Asia and is widely served in Singapore at Thai cuisine establishments. The texture contrasts crispy fried fish skin with tender, flaky white flesh and a sticky, flavourful sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate protein content from the sea bass (approximately 14 g per 100 g), with elevated fat from deep-frying and significant sugar and sodium from the sweet and sour sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), driven primarily by the sugar and starch in the sauce, but the glycemic load per serving is high due to the portion size. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the sugar load in the sauce and high glycemic load, and of high concern for hypertension due to sodium from fish sauce and condiments. The omega-3 fatty acids from sea bass offer cardiovascular benefit, partially offset by the frying method. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. Common serving size in Thai restaurants in Singapore is approximately 400 g for a shared main course.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for deep-fried sea bass with sweet and sour sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central cooked sea bass data with standard deep-frying oil absorption factors and sweet and sour sauce macro estimates from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate based on sweet and sour sauce GI literature. Medium confidence is appropriate for an estimated composite dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. USDA FoodData Central cooked sea bass (FDC 175159) was used as the protein and micronutrient base. Deep-frying oil absorption estimated at approximately 8–10% additional fat per 100 g fish. Sweet and sour sauce macros estimated from USDA SR Legacy sauce data. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for sea bass cooked by dry heat, and vegetable frying oils used in Thai cooking are not expected to contribute measurable trans fats. 4. GI set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is served at Sanook Kitchen - Funan (field survey data) at SGD 36.30. 6. The food_name has been canonicalized from the input '412D Deep-fried Sea Bass w Sweet and Sou' to 'Deep-Fried Sea Bass with Sweet and Sour Sauce'. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central sea bass and sauce component data and should be treated as approximate.

Added Chinese simplified and traditional local names.

Estimated GI at 55 from analogous sweet and sour sauce literature with full documentation.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect a glycemic_index of 55.
